statement.markdown: Given an array of integers `nums` and an integer `k`, return the maximum number of consecutive ones in `nums` if you can flip at most `k` zeros.
statement.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer n, the number of elements in the array.
The second line contains n space-separated integers representing the array `nums`.
The third line contains an integer `k`.
statement.outputFormat: The maximum number of consecutive ones.

constraints.description: Each element in `nums` is either 0 or 1.
The maximum number of zeros that can be flipped.

examples.explanation: You can flip the first zero to get 3 consecutive ones.
You can flip two zeros to get 6 consecutive ones.
You can flip two zeros to get 5 consecutive ones.

hints.text: Consider using a sliding window approach to track consecutive ones and zeros in the array.
Within the sliding window, maintain a count of zeros encountered. When this count exceeds k, adjust the window boundaries.
The maximum length of the window that can be achieved while having at most k zeros within it will give the maximum number of consecutive ones.

editorial.title: Max Consecutive Ones III: A Sliding Window Approach
editorial.intuition: The problem can be solved by finding the longest subarray with at most k zeros. A sliding window approach can be used to efficiently find this subarray.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would involve generating all possible subarrays and checking if each subarray has at most k zeros. This approach is correct but has a time complexity of O(n^2), making it slow for large inputs.
editorial.betterSolution: The optimal approach uses a sliding window with two pointers, left and right, to track the current subarray. The window is expanded to the right and shrunk from the left when the number of zeros exceeds k.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution has a time complexity of O(n) and a space complexity of O(1), making it efficient for large inputs.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the optimal solution can be proven by showing that the sliding window approach correctly tracks the maximum length subarray with at most k zeros. When the number of zeros exceeds k, the window is shrunk from the left, ensuring that the subarray always has at most k zeros.
editorial.dryRun: A dry run of the optimal solution with sample inputs can help verify its correctness and ensure that it handles edge cases correctly.
commonMistakes: Not properly updating the left pointer when the number of zeros exceeds k.
Not correctly calculating the maximum length subarray.
Failing to handle edge cases, such as an empty input array.
Not using a sliding window approach, leading to a less efficient solution.
Incorrectly assuming that the optimal solution requires more than O(1) space.

functionSignature.java: public static int solve(int[] nums, int k)

solutions.python:
import sys
it = iter(sys.stdin.read().split())
n = int(next(it))
nums = [int(next(it)) for _ in range(n)]
k = int(next(it))
max_len = 0
left = 0
zeros = 0
for right in range(n):
    if nums[right] == 0:
        zeros += 1
    while zeros > k:
        if nums[left] == 0:
            zeros -= 1
        left += 1
    max_len = max(max_len, right - left + 1)
print(max_len)
